Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I was going to sit down and study, but then I'd get distracted or just feel overwhelmed and not know where to start. With seven online courses, everything kind of blurred together. I'd usually just wait until something felt urgent enough to deal with, which meant a lot of last-minute scrambling.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and panic-study for hours. Regular times? That wasn't really my thing. My schedule depended entirely on what was about to be due and how scared I was of failing.
Q3: Usually it was fear, honestly. I'd get an email notification about a deadline or see something in a group chat that reminded me I was falling behind. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something catastrophic. It wasn't like I was excited to learn—I was just trying to avoid disaster.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me. I'd go through phases where I'd try to check in more regularly, but it never stuck. Life stuff would come up, or I'd just lose motivation, and then weeks would pass. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a bit with my anxiety, but they didn't make me more consistent with logging in.
Q5: The videos were the most useful because I'm a visual learner. I could watch them and actually understand concepts better than reading walls of text. I watched a decent amount of video content when I did engage. Sometimes the visual diagrams and demonstrations made things click in ways the written materials couldn't.
Q6: I ignored the discussion forums a lot. I only posted like five times the whole course. It felt awkward putting my thoughts out there, and I didn't see how it would help my grade directly. The readings I'd skim or skip entirely—I just didn't have the energy to go through everything when resources were tight and I was stretched thin.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be tested on or if it would help me pass an assignment. If I couldn't see an immediate payoff, I'd push it aside. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what would keep me from failing, not what might be 'nice to know.'
Q8: Optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it. I was barely keeping up with the mandatory work, so optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required assignments at least had consequences attached, which gave me enough fear to actually attempt them.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download PDFs and videos when I had good internet access, then study them offline later. My internet isn't always reliable, and I can't afford to waste data re-streaming things. A lot of my actual studying happened with downloaded materials, notes I'd written out by hand, and stuff I found on YouTube.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over my handwritten notes or rewatching downloaded lectures on my phone during commutes. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person sometimes, working through problems together, but the LMS had no idea that was happening.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video once, download it or take screenshots of key slides, and then never touch the LMS version again. It was more practical for me to have everything saved locally where I could access it without worrying about connectivity or burning through my data.
Q12: There were definitely low points. Around midterms, I was dealing with some family stuff and money stress, and everything felt impossible. I'd open the LMS and just stare at the screen, feeling scared and paralyzed. The workload from seven courses was crushing, and I couldn't figure out what to prioritize.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue was a huge factor—I was always tired. Confusion too, because with so many courses, I'd mix up what was due where. When I had to pick up extra hours at my job to cover expenses, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. Competing responsibilities won almost every time.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I was going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out. Those sessions felt pointless afterward.
Q15: I avoided the bigger assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The thought of starting them was overwhelming—I didn't know where to begin, and the fear of doing it wrong paralyzed me. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would become next week.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. When a deadline was close, I'd cram and rush through things, which is probably why my assignment completion was so low. I needed that external pressure, but it also made everything more stressful.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. When I got decent feedback, it motivated me a little to try harder next time. But sometimes feedback came too late to matter, or I was already so behind that it felt pointless. I didn't always check feedback right away because I was scared of what it might say.
Q18: The course structure was pretty rigid, which didn't work great for someone like me who was juggling a lot. Having seven courses with their own schedules and deadlines meant I was constantly playing catch-up. A more flexible structure might have helped me engage more consistently instead of in desperate bursts.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement harder. There was so much to get through that I'd feel defeated before starting. On the positive side, having videos helped since I learn better visually. But the lack of clear prioritization—what's essential versus what's supplementary—made it hard to know where to focus my limited energy.
Q20: I think that happens when people are trying to figure things out but struggling to produce anything. Like, you're clicking around, watching videos, reading stuff, but you can't translate that into completed work. Maybe they're confused, overwhelmed, or just don't know how to apply what they're seeing. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That's kind of me, actually. I barely showed up in the LMS but still passed. A lot of my studying happened offline or in ways the system couldn't track. I'd cram before exams using my downloaded materials and somehow scrape through. The LMS data would make me look more disengaged than I actually was.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were mentally present. They miss offline studying, emotional state, and real comprehension. A log might say I watched a video, but it can't tell you I was too stressed to absorb any of it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and invested. For me, real engagement was rare this semester. I was mostly just surviving, not thriving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how much I was dealing with outside of school. It wouldn't capture the offline studying, the stress about money, or the mental energy it took just to keep going. My low activity looks like apathy, but really it was overwhelm and fear holding me back.
